channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. at Fault in Strike on School in Iran, Preliminary Inquiry Says
item.description: Outdated targeting data may have resulted in a mistaken missile strike, according to the ongoing military investigation, which undercuts President Trump’s assertion that Iran could be to blame.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Eric Schmitt, Tyler Pager, Malachy Browne and Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: IRIB TV, via Agence France-Presse

item.title: How Trump and His Advisers Miscalculated Iran’s Response to War
item.description: In the lead-up to the U.S.-Israeli attack, President Trump downplayed the risks to the energy markets as a short-term concern that should not overshadow the mission to decapitate the Iranian regime.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
item.media:description: In response to Iranian threats, commercial shipping has come to a standstill in the Gulf, and oil prices have spiked.

item.title: How Trump Is Using the Paxton-Cornyn Race to Squeeze the Senate Over the SAVE Act
item.description: The president has yet to make an endorsement in the contest between John Cornyn and Ken Paxton as he tries to push the Senate to pass a bill requiring voters to show identification at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: New York Times photographs by Tamir Kalifa and Al Drago
item.media:description: President Trump is leveraging his potential endorsement in the Republican primary race for Senate between Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, and Senator John Cornyn to ratchet up the pressure on Senate Republicans to pass a bill that would impose strict voter ID requirements.

item.title: Republicans Concede They Need to Pivot on Immigration Before Midterms
item.description: In public comments and private meetings at a House G.O.P. retreat, top officials allowed that President Trump’s immigration crackdown had hurt the party and that they needed a course correction on the issue.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents making an arrest in Minneapolis in January. Speaker Mike Johnson told Republican lawmakers this week that some immigration enforcement was viewed as “overzealous.”

item.title: ICE Lawyer Who Told Judge She Was Overwhelmed Is Running for Congress
item.description: Julie T. Le, a former government lawyer, described in stark terms how overstretched the legal system had become during the administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. Now, she said, she hopes to fix the “system’s failures” by running for Congress.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Residents of Minneapolis using their phones to record federal agents last month.

item.title: James Fishback, a Republican Candidate for Florida Governor, Is Running on Rage Bait
item.description: Young conservatives in Florida are fascinated by James Fishback, a long-shot gubernatorial candidate known for his provocative online posts.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Fishback during an event at Luv’n Oven Ale House in Sunrise, Fla., in February. Even in person, Mr. Fishback, 31, is channeling a mode of politics that finds its energy through posting online.

item.title: Trump Documents Missing in Epstein Files Highlight DOJ’s Missteps
item.description: In late July, an F.B.I. agent asked colleagues to get started on a sensitive task relating to Jeffrey Epstein, listing the names of 14 prominent men, with President Trump at the top.
item.dc:creator: Steve Eder and Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A video showing Donald J. Trump with Jeffrey Epstein was displayed during Attorney General Pam Bondi’s testimony at a House Judiciary Committee hearing last month.

item.title: Starmer Was Warned of Mandelson’s Ties to Epstein Ahead of Ambassador Pick
item.description: Documents released by the U.K. government on Wednesday showed that Prime Minister Keir Starmer was told of Peter Mandelson’s ties to the sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Carl Court
item.media:description: Peter Mandelson and Prime Minister Keir Starmer in Washington, in February last year.

item.title: René Redzepi Steps Down at Noma Amid Allegations of Past Abuse
item.description: After 23 years running the Copenhagen restaurant, widely considered one of the most innovative and important in the world, the chef is leaving.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Thibault Savary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Redzepi led Noma to countless accolades, including being named No. 1 restaurant in the world five times by World’s 50 Best.

item.title: Noma Faces Los Angeles Protest Over Allegations of Past Abuse by Its Founder
item.description: At the vaunted restaurant’s pop-up in the Silver Lake neighborhood, fallout from recent reports continued.
item.dc:creator: Meghan McCarron and Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the protest he helped organize, Jason Ignacio White, center, read from an open letter to Noma.

item.title: Why Falling Cats Always Seem to Land on Their Feet
item.description: It takes backbone to solve an enigma like the “falling cat” problem.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Mitchell Brown

item.title: Washington State Passes ‘Millionaires’ Tax’
item.description: It would be the first income tax in Washington, affecting an estimated 20,000 households. Some of the wealthiest are leaving for Florida.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Howard Schultz, the former Starbucks chief executive, said on social media that he was moving to Florida.

item.title: Trump Targets Major Trading Partners With New Investigation
item.description: A trade investigation focuses on the European Union, China, India and other countries and will likely lead to tariffs to replace those struck down by the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tyler Pager
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: The trade investigations would be carried out under a law that allows the United States to impose tariffs on foreign countries in response to certain foreign practices.

item.title: Strong or Weak? How Trump Picks His Battles.
item.description: Despite his tough talk, President Trump has consistently made allowances for countries he sees as powerful or dominant.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia during a meeting in Alaska last summer. Mr. Trump’s deference to Russia highlights a pattern that has crystallized in recent months as he launches military action overseas.

item.title: How to Afford Housing in London: Multiple Roommates, No Living Room
item.description: High prices have pushed more Londoners to live with roommates well into their late 20s and 30s — if they can afford to move out of their parents’ homes at all.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Darragh pays 900 pounds, or about $1,225, a month for his share of a six-bedroom house in a northwest London neighborhood.

item.title: Hawaii Storm Bringing Flooding, Fierce Winds and Even Snow
item.description: The storm, called a kona low, is expected to churn slowly across the islands all week.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: Ron Sachs/CNP, via Reuters
item.media:description: Forecasters have said a severe storm will slowly cross the islands of Hawaii this week.

item.title: At Least 2 Killed as Tornadoes Hit Illinois and Indiana
item.description: At least two people were killed and several were injured in the severe storm on Tuesday that heavily damaged areas of Illinois and Indiana.
item.dc:creator: Robert Chiarito, Sonia A. Rao and Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: Armando L. Sanchez/Chicago Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A destroyed home in Aroma Park, Ill., part of Kankakee County, on Wednesday.

item.title: Slavery Photos of Renty Get a ‘Final Resting Place,’ Ending a Fight With Harvard
item.description: The images of a father known as Renty and his daughter Delia were honored today in a ceremony by their new steward, a museum in South Carolina.
item.dc:creator: Clyde McGrady
item.media:credit: Donaven Doughty for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Tonya Matthews, left, president of the International African American Museum in Charleston, S.C., walking with Tamara Lanier, the woman who says she is a descendant of the subjects in the slavery photos, on the museum grounds on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Isn’t Ready for What He’s Starting in Cuba
item.description: Catastrophe in Cuba is not a foregone conclusion.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Sabatini and Katrin Hansing
item.media:credit: Ramon Espinosa/Associated Press

item.title: Carolyn Bessette Was Living the Dream. Then She Met John.
item.description: The fairy tale was 1990s New York.
item.dc:creator: Glynnis MacNicol
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Addresses the Notion of a Drone Strike on California
item.description: “Isn’t this how ‘Ironman 3’ started?” Kimmel said after the F.B.I. warned state officials tobb prepare for a retaliatory Iranian drone strike on the West Coast ahead of Sunday’s Academy Awards.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: A Third of Americans Have Cut Spending or Borrowed Money for Health Care
item.description: As medical costs rise, more than 80 million people have made sacrifices like skipping meals and driving less, a new survey finds.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.media:credit: Arin Yoon/Reuters
item.media:description: A recent poll found that health care costs topped a list of the public’s economic anxieties, above concerns about the prices of food and groceries, gas and utilities.

item.title: Top-End Auction Sales Help Pull Global Art Market Out of Slump, Study Says
item.description: The top drivers included a $2.2 billion auction week in New York and strong fall fairs, according to the annual Art Basel and UBS report.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Art Basel Paris fair at the Grand Palais last October.

item.title: Angelika Saleh, the Angelika of Angelika Film Center, Dies at 90
item.description: After making the journey from prewar Germany to Madison Avenue opulence, she gave her name to one of New York’s most influential indie cinemas.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: Ex-Officer Who Took Nude Images From Phones in Traffic Stops Is Sentenced
item.description: The former Missouri police officer, Julian Alcala, was sentenced to two years in prison and now faces civil lawsuits from several of the 20 victims the authorities identified.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Bryan Birks for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Thomas F. Eagleton U.S. courthouse in St. Louis, where a former Missouri police officer, Julian Alcala, was sentenced on Wednesday.
